message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>Better understanding of the spatiotemporal structure of the COVID-19 epidemic in the USA may help inform more effective prevention and control strategies. By analyzing daily COVID-19 case data in the United States, Mexico and Canada, we found four continental-scale epidemic wave patterns, including travelling waves, that spanned multiple state and even international boundaries. These major epidemic patterns co-varied strongly with continental-scale seasonal temperature change patterns. Geo-contiguous states shared similar timing and amplitude of epidemic wave patterns irrespective of similarities or differences in state government political party affiliations. These analyses provide evidence that seasonal factors, probably weather changes, have exerted major effects on local COVID-19 incidence rates. Seasonal wave patterns observed during the first year of the epidemic may become repeated in the subsequent years.</jats:p>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>One Sentence Summary</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The COVID-19 epidemic in the United States has consisted of four continental-scale spatiotemporal waves of case incidence that have spanned multiple states and even international boundaries.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Prominent Spatiotemporal Waves of COVID-19 Incidence in the United States: Implications for Causality, Forecasting, and Control

reference.unstructured: WHO., Coronavirus disease (COVID-19) pandemic. Available from https://www.who.int/emergencies/diseases/novel-coronavirus-2019?gclid=EAIaIQobChMIrb-76uy38QIVt21vBB1Vmw6fEAAYASAAEgIHB_D_BwE. Accessed on June 20, 2021. (2021).
